dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 907 responding to a male with a seizure on a sidewalk in [CITY].
Good afternoon, sir. I'm Michael, an EMT with Rescue 907. What happened to you today?
I had a seizure about 45 minutes ago and ever since I've been confused.
Your eyes are open. Can you follow my finger with your eyes?
You seem confused. What is your name, and where are you today?
If you can, squeeze my hand with yours to show us you can follow directions.
Can you breathe on your own?
We're going to monitor your breathing and oxygen levels now.
Respiratory rate is 14 breaths per minute.
Oxygen saturation is 97 percent.
Blood pressure is 155 over 76.
Pulse is 89 beats per minute.
Your skin is normal, warm, and slightly clammy.
You mentioned a history of seizures. Can you describe symptoms leading up to this episode?
You mentioned a history of seizures. Are you on medication for it?
You are taking Depakote. Have you missed a dose recently?
Would you mind telling me how long you've been outside?
I'll now inspect your skin for signs of trauma or bruising.
Blood glucose is reading 126 mg/dL.
We will now secure a 4 lead ECG to look at your heart rhythm.
Heart rhythm is currently showing normal sinus rhythm.
Applying a 12 lead ECG now for detailed heart monitoring.
The 12 lead results show a nonspecific intraventricular conduction block.
Pulse has decreased slightly to 88 beats per minute.
Current blood pressure is 148 over 75.
Oxygen saturation has increased to 99 percent.
Glucose is currently not detecting a reading, even after retesting.
I will reconnect the 4 lead ECG for continuous monitoring.
EKG continues to show regular sinus rhythm.
Based on your history, altered mental status, and nonspecific ECG findings, we are proceeding with seizure protocol 3-12.
Pupils are equal and reactive.
You are awake and oriented to person, but less so to time and place.
Pulse is 86 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 157 over 76.
Oxygen saturation is at 95 percent.
Glucose remains non-detectable.
Are you currently experiencing any pain or discomfort?
You mentioned you are homeless and have no insurance. Has your past experience with seizures been managed on a regular basis?
We'll be transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] for further neurological and medical evaluation. Is that okay?
We will assist you safely to the stretcher and secure you for transportation.
Reapplying the 4 lead monitor to track your rhythm during movement.
Respiratory rate remains at 14 breaths per minute.
Oxygen saturation is maintained at 95 percent.
We'll now make you comfortable and begin the ambulance loading process.
Pulse is 86 and stable.
Blood pressure remains 157 over 76.
Your breath sounds are clear and symmetrical. No signs of distress are noted.
Let's check your motor response again. Can you squeeze my hand on command? I see you are able to do so.
You mentioned you are taking Depakote regularly. Have you had side effects or complications with it before?
You're now on board and ready. Our ambulance is starting transportation back to the hospital.
We're proceeding to [COMPANY_NAME] and will monitor you en route.
During transport, we'll perform repeated assessments to monitor your mental status and vitals continuously.
Your skin is still warm.
Based on the GCS of 14 and altered mental status, we're monitoring for potential progression of neurological symptoms.
Oxygen saturation remains at 95 percent.
Are there any specific concerns you would like to communicate to the ER team at the hospital?
We are monitoring you continuously during transport without any observed decline at this time.
Our patient is en route now with all clinical documentation ready for handoff at [COMPANY_NAME].
Your personal effects will travel with you in the ambulance today.
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] has been arranged and all hospital communications have been made.
Final EKG reading still shows normal sinus rhythm with conduction abnormalities.
Final blood pressure recorded is 157 over 76.
Recommend protocol 3-12 for continued management with the hospital neurology team.
The patient is alert, able to follow simple commands, but still confused and disoriented.
No visible signs of pain or discomfort at this time. Your Glasgow Coma Scale by our assessment is 14.
There were no episodes of active seizure during our assessment, and your mental status showed some improvement during transport.
Your last glucose reading was non-detectable, but we will note for the ER this potential issue during transport.
Our team documented three vital sign assessments with consistent arterial findings.
You were noted to have a history of status epilepticus in the past. Is there a risk of recurrence?
Please lay still while we secure your belongings in the ambulance for transport.
Based on the altered mental status and history of epilepsy, your assessment is consistent with status epilepticus definition.
All monitoring devices are secured and continuous data has been captured for the hospital team.
Your pain assessment remains zero at the current time.
Your belongings are now ready for transport. All is in order for your hospital arrival.
No chest discomfort or dizziness was reported through the interview and self-assessment.
No sensory feelings such as tinnitus or visual changes were reported during neurological checks.
If any warning signs such as sudden dizziness should occur during transport, let us know immediately.
The airway was found to be patent per standard neurological protocols.
You were found to be at 3 out of 4 on mental status, but your alertness increased during transport to 4 of 4.
All monitoring leads are secured, and initial 12 lead ECG findings have been transmitted.
Your status epilepticus history was noted, and hospital staff will know to prepare for potential seizure events per protocol.
As per the ePCR, you were found on a walker and have no known drug allergies or sensory symptoms noted.
No evidence of trauma, rashes, or skin bruises were noted on visual inspection today.
All of your checks, from head to toe, are pending further neurological input from specialists at [COMPANY_NAME].
I will now double-check your comfort within the transport module.
Patient was not observed in any acute distress, and no significant vital deviations have occurred since initial assessment.
We are approximately ten minutes from the hospital, and response teams are aware of our arrival.
Let me just confirm we have all of your medication and identification details for the record.
As per this shift's focus on neurological emergency, all documentation has been prioritized for review upon patient arrival.
Your identification, including an ID by name only, has been documented along with your transfer from the walker to the unit.
Your temperature, skin color, and skin condition are all within normal range despite the ongoing mental status concern.
No signs of any immediate orthopedic anomalies such as pain on palpation have been recorded.
You were found to be awake and responsive at the start of this event.
I will now close this section of our medical report for this patient transport event.
All documented clinical and procedural requirements have been met for protocol completion.
You had a GCS 14 and were found not in active convulsion or under alert conditions at first contact by us.
Reiterate that you are not experiencing chest discomfort or dizziness post-presentation.
You have no known drug allergy as per the records given now.
You are in a position now. If you feel any specific sensations or become more alert, let us know. Otherwise, stay relaxed.
Your mental status and progress are expectedly per protocol for potential status epilepticus patient transport.
Your final check with 2019-08-13t15:59:41 shows continued stability in cardiovascular and mental domains.
No additional interventions will be performed at this point in transport according to initial protocol.
The hospital has accepted all documentation generated as part of this early EMS intervention.
Final notes include no chest pain, dizziness, or compounds of distress for a transport of marginally altered mind.
Thank you for your cooperation and trust in our process, sir. The team is ready and en route.
